items.myth: GELU is just ReLU with a fancy name.
items.reality: GELU uses x·Φ(x), a smooth probabilistic gate; ReLU is max(0,x) with a hard zero for negatives and a nondifferentiable corner at zero.
items.example: At x = -1, ReLU output is 0; GELU output is about -0.16—non-zero and smooth, not a clipped ReLU.

items.myth: GELU always beats ReLU on every architecture.
items.reality: GELU became dominant in Transformers after strong NLP results, but ReLU remains common in CNNs and many efficient pipelines where the extra compute does not pay off.
items.example: A ResNet-style vision baseline often trains fine with ReLU; switching to GELU without retuning may yield no gain and adds per-element math cost.

items.myth: The sigmoid form x·σ(1.702x) is the exact definition of GELU.
items.reality: That sigmoid expression is a convenient approximation; the canonical definition is x times the standard normal CDF, implemented exactly via the error function.
items.example: PyTorch `GELU(approximate='none')` uses erf; `approximate='tanh'` uses the BERT tanh shortcut—both approximate the same underlying Φ(x) gate.
